---
title: "How to read a discrete choice experiment"
description: "A short, non-technical guide to what a DCE can and cannot tell you."
author: "Mesfin Genie"
date: "2026-06-20"
categories: [methods, choice modelling]
---

A discrete choice experiment (DCE) asks people to choose between options described by several attributes. From those choices we estimate how much each attribute matters, and the trade-offs people are willing to make.

Three things are worth keeping in mind when you read one.

First, a DCE measures stated preferences, not behaviour. It is a well-designed hypothetical, which is a strength for options that do not yet exist, and a limitation to keep in view.

Second, the headline is usually the trade-off, not a single number. The interesting result is how much of one attribute people will give up for another, for example how much waiting time they will accept for a lower cost.

Third, averages hide variation. Preference heterogeneity, how much preferences differ across people, is often the most policy-relevant finding, because it shows who is affected and how.

I will expand on each of these in later posts, with examples from health and public policy.
